On that note, let's crank it down on this beautiful morning. I have work to do. You have work to do. Many of you have another day of retirement to live. Go out and keep busy. I watch the retiree across the street from us, John, out working on his new Ford Maverick truck just about every day. He's waxed down that orange truck at least five times in 10 days.

Hey, at least he's staying busy. Let's go get after it.

item.title: Mike Waltz confronts leftist Democrat over Cuba trip after Marco Rubio jab: 'His entire family is from Cuba'
item.description: Rep. Mark Pocan claimed Marco Rubio's Cuba views carry less weight because he has never visited the island, sparking fierce backlash from critics.
item.content:encoded:
A leftist Democrat sparked fierce backlash after he appeared to insist that Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s views on Cuba carried less weight because he has not visited the island.

Rep. Mark Pocan, D-Wis., made the striking claim during a heated exchange with U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz on Wednesday at a committee hearing.

Pocan, who has compared Cuba to a "silent Gaza," visited the communist island earlier in July, where he called for the U.S. to relent on its pressure campaign against the Cuban government, blaming U.S. sanctions for the island's economic devastation and fuel shortages.

During his questioning of Waltz, he did not address the Cuban government's likely hoarding of resources as it maintains a firm grip over the economy or its continued access to luxuries amid economic turmoil.

TRUMP EXPANDS CUBA SANCTIONS BEYOND US COMPANIES IN MAJOR CRACKDOWN ON FOREIGN ENABLERS

"During your visit, did you visit the private island from the Castro regime?" Waltz asked Pocan. "Did you visit their private jet which seems to have fuel to get back and forth?"

Pocan dodged the question and fired back, "Considering you've never been to Cuba, I'm not going to take your advice on where to go."

"Marco Rubio has never been to Cuba, so many others who have strong opinions," the Wisconsin lawmaker added later.

"His entire family is from Cuba and fled the regime," Waltz replied.

"They left before the revolution," Pocan responded. "That is decades ago. That’s a ridiculous answer. It’s worth going there if you’re going to have such strong opinions and I think you’d maybe look at it a little differently."

The exchange drew swift criticism on social media, with several conservative commentators accusing Pocan of parroting the Cuban government's talking points.

"It is completely insane that @RepMarkPocan is using a Congressional hearing to shill for the communist regime in Cuba," Cody Sargent, communications director for Department of Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, wrote on social media. "Americans need to see how absolutely crazy @TheDemocrats have become."

CUBA BUILT COVERT NETWORK INSIDE AMERICA THROUGH SPIES, ACTIVISTS AND UNIVERSITIES, STATE DEPT ALLEGES

"Mark Pocan (who consistently makes a strong case for dumbest member of Congress) proves once again how he fails to have a functioning brain," National Republican Congressional Committee spokesman Mike Marinella wrote Wednesday. "This exchange is exactly the kind of thinking communist regimes love to cultivate."

"A perfect example of what Rubio’s report on Cuba warned about re: Communist Cuba’s influence and espionage in America ," NewsNation anchor Katie Pavlich observed.

Pavlich referenced findings the State Department released earlier this week detailing a decades-long influence campaign the Cuban government allegedly pushed within the United States to sow division and push Communist ideas.

"From its earliest years in power, the Cuban regime has believed that its own survival and strategic interests were inextricably linked to the advance of the revolutionary left inside the United States," the report states in part.

Pocan notably dismissed the report as "ridiculous," arguing Rubio issued the document as a pretext for regime change on the island.

"It may be the stupidest, most fantastical, most biased thing I’ve ever read from a governmental agency," Pocan said in a statement released Tuesday.

Pocan also pressed Waltz on whether the administration plans to pursue military action against Cuba. The top diplomat declined to take any option off the table for the president.

Fox News Digital reached out to Pocan for comment.

item.title: AI builds virtual homes where robots learn from their mistakes
item.description: SceneSmith uses AI agents powered by GPT-5.2 to build detailed 3D environments where robots practice tasks and expose faulty plans before real homes.
item.content:encoded:
Ask a robot to put a coffee mug in the cabinet, and you quickly see how complicated an ordinary chore can become. You know where the mug belongs. You can step around a chair, avoid the clutter on the counter and open the right cabinet without giving it much thought. A robot has to work through every part of that process.

That helps explain why today's robots can look impressive during a demonstration but still struggle with basic jobs around a home or factory. They need experience in many different rooms and situations before they can work reliably. Teaching them everything in the physical world takes time and plenty of hands-on supervision.

Researchers at MIT's Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory and Toyota Research Institute believe virtual training could help. Their system, called SceneSmith, uses AI agents to create detailed 3D indoor environments from simple text prompts. Robots can then practice tasks and expose weak action plans inside those AI-generated spaces before they enter a real home or workplace.

Sign up for my FREE CyberGuy Report

WOULD YOU TRUST A TINY DENTAL ROBOT?

Robots learn through experience. However, engineers cannot easily stage every room layout or object arrangement a machine may encounter. Physical testing also requires someone to reset the scene after each attempt. A tipped chair or misplaced bottle can change the next test. Meanwhile, a failed movement may damage the robot or something nearby.

Simulation offers a safer alternative. A robot can repeat a task without breaking a dish or tying up a real workspace. Still, many earlier simulation systems created rooms that felt sparse. They often lacked the clutter, movable furniture and physical details found in everyday spaces. SceneSmith makes those training environments feel much closer to the real world.

Other developers are also using AI video technology to speed up humanoid robot training, turning small amounts of real footage into synthetic training environments.

SceneSmith starts with a simple written request. A researcher could ask for a garage with a car and workbench, then add tires in the corner and a ladder against the wall. From there, three AI agents powered by GPT-5.2 work together to build the space. A designer starts creating the room. A critic checks whether everything looks realistic. An orchestrator manages the back-and-forth and decides when the design is ready.

SceneSmith builds each environment one layer at a time. It starts with the floor plan and furniture, then adds objects to the walls and ceiling. Smaller items that a robot can pick up or move come last. The critic also catches choices that feel out of place. For example, it might suggest removing a bathtub from the living room. The orchestrator can even send the project back a few steps when part of the design needs more work.

Once the agents agree, SceneSmith adds the physics that control how objects move and react. The result gives researchers a working virtual room where a robot can open cabinets and handle objects instead of a 3D scene that only looks realistic.

A robot needs more than realistic scenery. It needs objects that respond when touched. SceneSmith can place cabinets with working doors. It can also create movable objects and estimate physical properties such as mass or friction. Those details affect how an item reacts when a robot grabs, pushes or drops it.

For standard objects, the system can use a text-to-image-to-3D process. It retrieves articulated items with moving parts from a curated library. This approach keeps cabinet doors and drawers functional inside the simulator. The system also checks for overlapping objects and lets gravity settle them into stable positions.

Researchers reported that 96% of objects remained stable during simulation. Fewer than 2% of object pairs collided with one another. That work matters because a robot cannot learn much from a virtual kitchen where cups float or furniture sinks into the floor.

Better virtual practice could also support advances like this new AI brain that helps robots move more like humans, especially when a task requires precise hand movements.

HUMANOID ROBOT CLEANS FIRST US APARTMENT

The researchers used SceneSmith to generate more than 1,300 scenes. The collection included familiar spaces such as bedrooms and hotels. It also produced unusual settings, including a pottery store and a Minecraft-themed gaming room. Some scenes contained up to six times more items than those from earlier methods. As a result, robots can encounter the type of clutter that makes real-world tasks difficult.

A robot might need to move a soda can from a shelf to a table. Another task could involve placing a cup in the sink. Each scene changes the surroundings, so the robot cannot depend on one carefully staged layout. That variety gives engineers a better way to see whether a robot's plan works beyond a single demonstration.

In robotics, a policy tells a machine how to act based on what it observes. SceneSmith gives researchers a way to test those policies across many environments. The team generated 100 evaluation scenes covering four manipulation tasks. A robot policy attempted each chore in simulation. Then, an AI evaluator checked the result using simulator data and visual observations.

The evaluator reached 99.7% agreement with human labels. That result suggests researchers could use the system to screen robot attempts at scale without manually judging every run. The headline's phrase "learn from their mistakes" needs one important distinction. SceneSmith reveals where a policy fails. Engineers can use those failures to revise later versions of the robot's behavior. In this study, the system focused on scene generation and automatic evaluation rather than continuous self-retraining after every mistake.

A virtual room can look realistic to a person and still behave poorly during robot training. Therefore, the team tested SceneSmith through physical interaction inside the simulator. Researchers placed an independently trained robot policy into generated scenes. The policy predated SceneSmith and had never trained on its environments or assets.

In one demonstration, the robot moved an apple from a bowl to a cutting board after receiving a language instruction. The team also teleoperated a robot through the virtual spaces. Researchers guided it as it opened cabinets and put away bottles. These tests showed that the environments could support more than visual inspection. Together, the demonstrations provide early evidence that SceneSmith's rooms can support useful robotics experiments.

People preferred SceneSmith's virtual rooms

The researchers asked 205 people to compare SceneSmith with earlier scene-generation methods. SceneSmith recorded an average 92% win rate for realism. It also earned a 91% average win rate for following the original prompt.

In practical terms, participants usually thought its rooms looked more believable and matched the request more closely. Those results support the visual side of the system. However, robot performance remains the more important test. SceneSmith's ability to combine realistic design with usable physics gives the project its strongest advantage.

ROBOT SOCCER PLAYER DENTS WALL WITH TERRIFYING KICKS

Detailed virtual rooms take time to build. MIT says SceneSmith may need several hours to produce one scene because the AI agents create and inspect many objects. More computing power could speed up that process. Even so, generating a large training library would require substantial resources.

The current system also has limited support for deformable objects such as sponges. These materials change shape when touched, which makes them harder to reproduce in simulation. The researchers hope larger 3D libraries will help them expand that capability.

Real-world testing will still play a vital role. Homes contain unpredictable people and objects that wear down or break. Simulation can reduce risky trial and error, but engineers must still prove that robots behave safely outside a digital room.

A future household robot could practice inside hundreds or even thousands of virtual rooms before it ever rolls into your kitchen. That gives developers more chances to catch a bad move or weak action plan while the robot remains inside a simulator. SceneSmith can also expose a robot to rooms filled with different furniture, objects and layouts. That matters because your kitchen will look nothing like the carefully arranged space where a robot first learned a task.

That kind of preparation could eventually help machines like this home robot designed to cook, clean and handle the clutter and surprises found in everyday homes.

The technology remains in the research stage, and robots will still need plenty of testing in the physical world. Still, SceneSmith tackles a problem robot makers need to solve: How do you prepare a machine for homes where chairs move, counters get cluttered and almost nothing stays in the same place?

SceneSmith gives robots a safe place to get things wrong. Engineers can spot a bad move inside a crowded virtual home before the machine risks damaging something or moving too close to a person. What makes this system interesting is that the rooms actually work like physical spaces. Cabinets open, objects move and robots can interact with what is around them. The biggest drawback is speed. Building one detailed scene can take hours. Still, these AI-generated environments could help developers catch more problems before a robot ever enters your home.

Would you trust a household robot more if it had already made thousands of mistakes inside AI-generated homes? Let us know by writing to us at Cyberguy.com

Sign up for my FREE CyberGuy Report

Copyright 2026 CyberGuy.com. All rights reserved.

item.title: Comedian was 'broke' in $3M house after water shut off during divorce
item.description: Laura Clery's memoir details allegations against ex-husband Stephen Hilton, from cheating and drug addiction to threats that led to their custody battle.
item.content:encoded:
Laura Clery is giving fans an inside look into some of her darker moments in her memoir.

In her new memoir, "What Doesn't Kill You Makes You Hotter," the 39-year-old actress and comedian opened up about her relationship with her ex-husband, Stephen Hilton, and their tumultuous separation.

The "Shanked" star described the day she found out Hilton had allegedly been cheating on her by visiting massage parlors that seemingly offered "happy endings." She explained that "it was hard not to take it personally," as they had just been intimate earlier that day, and even though it left her feeling she wasn't "enough," she writes what she "mainly felt was hurt, and empathy toward him."

"It wouldn’t be my lack that drove Stephen to a stranger," she alleged in her memoir. "It would be his. It would be the thing we’d both tried to fill with drugs and alcohol and sex—the hole in the soul that, if we weren’t careful, could take us down to the rock bottom we both knew so well. I decided that I would meet him with grace and love and empathy, because if he was spiraling, the last thing he would need is to feel alone."

LAUREN SILVERMAN BREAKS SILENCE ON GETTING PREGNANT WITH SIMON COWELL'S CHILD WHILE MARRIED TO SOMEONE ELSE

After Hilton returned home, Clery allegedly confronted him, noting she was initially met with apologies and promises to call his sponsor, which left her feeling as if "Stephen and I could manage this challenge together" and eventually "move forward."

Later that night, however, she claims her ex-husband called her and allegedly began hurling insults at her, telling her, "I don’t have a problem. This is your fault! You’re just so f---ing controlling!" Following the phone call, she checked his location and found he was allegedly at yet another massage parlor.

"If I could say anything to my then-self, I’d tell her to buckle up," Clery wrote. "Change the locks. Call your mom, call your lawyer, call your best friend. Believe the voice inside your head when it tells you that something is wrong. Be smart, be brave, and you’ll get through this."

The following day, Clery recalled a family trip to Malibu, during which Hilton allegedly began screaming in front of their young children that he planned to take his life. She alleged that he screamed "I’m going to kill myself, and it’s your f---ing fault!" He then left the house and Clery called the police, but once they found him, he presented as "a man who was absolutely fine—a man with a crazy, overdramatic wife."

She claims that she woke up the next day to messages asking if she was OK, and discovered Hilton had taken to social media and shared details of the incident with his followers. She says he allegedly didn't seem to care that she was upset about his videos, helping her realize he didn't care about salvaging their relationship, and that they needed time apart. She also alleged she later found out that Hilton had relapsed in his drug addiction at this time.

"I realized in a flash of clarity—truly, it felt like life b-----slapping me in the face—that I had deluded myself into thinking that the worst was over," she wrote. "I knew in my bones that we were a ways from better days. We had to separate."

From there, Clery writes that the separation evolved into years of legal disputes, financial hardship and a painful process of rebuilding her life as a single mother.

Clery alleges Hilton later broke into her home, leading to his arrest, hacked her social media accounts and launched an online community that portrayed her as Satan, claims she says further damaged both her finances and emotional well-being.

The couple announced their separation in August 2022 and filed for divorce in June 2023. In the memoir, Clery also goes into detail about her financial struggles following their separation and divorce.

She recalled staying in mediation with Hilton for too long, because she "was scared to let go of my creative partner" and "scared to stop sharing the bills with him," but also thought that by continuing to work with him, she could help him, but noted, "You can’t pay your water bill with good intentions."

"That fear cost me two years and so much money," she wrote. "Thousands and thousands of dollars gone, until I didn’t have the money left to even keep the endless mediation going. Maybe that was Stephen’s plan all along. Maybe he wanted me to bleed out financially. And maybe I played right into it."

JAMIE-LYNN SIGLER CLAIMS HER EX-HUSBAND SECRETLY STOLE HUNDREDS OF THOUSANDS FROM HER 'SOPRANOS' EARNINGS

Clery described "living from massive paycheck to massive paycheck," claiming she was paying both her mortgage and Hilton's mortgage, along with two years of mediation.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

She explained that during their marriage "Stephen handled the money," and after her water was shut off one evening, she "realized I had handed away my power with every bill I refused to look at."

"My water got shut off. In my three-million-dollar house. Yes, you read that correctly. I was broke. In a three-million-dollar house," she wrote. "Having my water shut off? Yeah, that was my bottom. But it was a lesson I needed to learn. Look at the d--- bills. Face the scary thing. Because if you keep ignoring it, you’ll get something much scarier than dry taps and overflowing toilets."

In the midst of their separation, Clery endured false allegations against her that she abused her children, but ultimately was awarded full custody, with Hilton getting supervised visitation, but she is hopeful about his recovery.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

"One day, maybe, Alfie and Poppy will be able to spend time with their dad without court supervision. Maybe Stephen will stay sober. To his credit, by mid-November, he passed one hundred days of sobriety. Maybe this chapter of our lives might finally be coming to a close. Maybe. Time will tell," she concluded. "For now, my guard is up, my boundaries are firm, and I’m cautiously inching toward hope again."

Hilton has publicly discussed his struggles with addiction and his mental health in the years since their separation.

item.title: Owe child support? The Trump administration may revoke your passport
item.description: Parents have paid nearly $1.5 million in past-due child support since the Trump administration began revoking passports for those owing $50,000 or more.
item.content:encoded:
A parent’s passport can be revoked if they fail to pay child support thanks to a new Trump administration enforcement policy. The tool has proven effective in putting children first, compelling many noncustodial parents to immediately pay thousands in overdue child support, some making single payments of upwards of $100,000.

Since 1996, the State Department, in partnership with the Office of Child Support Enforcement at the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), has used the Passport Denial Program as a mechanism to compel child support payments. The program focused on denying new passport applications and renewals for parents owing $2,500 or more in child support.

While our office has found this has been effective, generating more than $660 million in reported collections since its inception, passports only need to be renewed every 10 years. That means the program has missed opportunities in many cases to compel immediate action — until now.

In May, the Administration for Children and Families launched an initiative in collaboration with the State Department to actively revoke passports held by individuals owing substantial child support arrearages. Now, passports are being revoked for parents who owe $50,000 or more in child support, and there are plans to lower this threshold. The program has already compelled a number of noncustodial parents to make large child support payments.

FLIGHT PASSENGER BLASTS 'INCOMPETENT PARENTS' AFTER UNRULY CHILD FORCES PLANE DELAY

A father in Nevada wanted to take his son abroad this summer to celebrate the young man’s graduation from high school. But the father’s passport had been revoked due to his failure to pay child support. No problem — the dad made a $119,000 child support payment to receive his new passport and take his son abroad.

A Connecticut noncustodial parent needed to travel to Europe for a business trip, so he made a nearly $180,000 child support payment and secured his passport. After not making a child support payment since 2021, a parent in Maryland paid over $100,000 after receiving a passport revocation notice. A dad in Montana paid $147,000 in past-due child support to a mother of four children. Another parent in Arizona paid their full balance, plus interest, of over $170,000. A father in Texas made a $16,000 payment to ensure he did not lose his passport privilege. After seeing media coverage of the new passport revocation action, another father in Texas preemptively made a $6,000 child support payment.

DHS ISSUES $84B IN FINES TO ILLEGAL IMMIGRANTS UNDER TRUMP ADMINISTRATION

In total, parents have paid nearly $1.5 million in past-due child support since the new passport policy was implemented in May, and the State Department has revoked more than 6,000 passports and continues to revoke about 1,000 a week. While the law has long allowed for passports to be revoked for failure to make child support payments, enforcement was not undertaken because it was considered burdensome and required extensive collaboration between the State Department and the Office of Child Support Enforcement at HHS.

For the Trump administration, however, the benefits — not only to a parent raising children, but to the furtherment of a responsible society — outweigh logistics concerns.

For many Americans, passports are critical to their livelihood and ability to travel for personal reasons, which is why revocation is such an effective tool. The message from the Trump administration is clear: if you haven’t paid what you owe to your children, we are going to prevent you from using your passport, which is a privilege, not a right.

In addition to leveraging passports to compel payments, the Office of Child Support Enforcement assists child support agencies in collecting past-due support through a variety of mechanisms, including federal tax refund offsets, liens and levies against property and financial accounts, and insurance claim intercepts.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

Child support programs also use tools like property seizures, and driver’s license and professional license suspension to collect delinquent support, but the most common enforcement mechanism is income withholding from a noncustodial parent’s employer. In 2024, 74% of child support collected was from an employee paycheck.

The administrative financial investment in child support collection programs has also proven to be highly cost-effective. In 2024, the child support program collected $4.24 for every $1 spent, according to the Office of Child Support Enforcement.

The Trump administration is prioritizing the needs of children and families, and the Office of Child Support Enforcement will use every available tool to ensure non-paying parents are held accountable.

Alex J. Adams is the assistant secretary for the Administration for Children and Families.

item.title: Fox News Power Rankings: The top races to watch as 36 states choose their next governor
item.description: From Wisconsin's toss-up race to Kansas Republicans' best flip opportunity, these governor races could decide which party holds a majority of statehouses.
item.content:encoded:
The battle to control Congress always dominates midterm discussions, but should it?

More voters will elect a governor than a U.S. senator this year.

Among the key contests are what might be the GOP’s only flip opportunity, races that will show how key voter groups are responding to President Donald Trump’s second term, and even a test for Democrats in the Northwest.

These elections could also decide which party holds a majority of governorships. More than half of the nation’s governors have been Republicans since 2010. But with Democrats currently holding 24 of the 50 offices, that advantage is two states away from disappearing.

TOP DEMOCRAT GOVERNORS ARE HOPING TO RIDE THEIR RECORDS TO THE WHITE HOUSE. CAN THEY?

Here are the top races to watch this year.

Democrats have been going to war with each other over ideology, character and electability all across the country this year – in Wisconsin, the party is fighting all three battles in one race.

At least seven notable candidates are vying for the nomination, including Democratic socialist and state Rep. Francesca Hong and former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes, who lost a U.S. Senate race here four years ago.

This week, the primary got even messier. Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez ended her run after discovering she was short hundreds of thousands of dollars, and nine days after suspending his run, county executive David Crowley got back in, touting an endorsement from Gov. Tony Evers.

TRUMP-BACKED FIREBRAND WINS ARIZONA GOP GOVERNOR PRIMARY, SETTING UP HIGH-STAKES SHOWDOWN

Wisconsin is as competitive as it gets. It’s one of only two states that has voted for both parties for president and the Senate while also being run by governors from both parties during the Trump era (the other is Arizona).

While the Democrats figure out who their candidate is, Republicans have a clear frontrunner: U.S. House Rep. Tom Tiffany, who has Trump’s backing and $3 million in the bank.

It’s too early to say which party will have the edge in November. This state is a toss-up.

Iowa’s Senate race is a toss-up, as Tuesday’s Power Rankings revealed, but on the governor’s side, the Democrats have opened up quite a lead.

Rob Sand, the Democratic state auditor who claims he’s not a fan of either party, had the support of 53% of voters in the latest Fox News Poll, nine points ahead of his Republican opponent, Zach Lahn, a local farmer.

Lahn was the surprise winner of his party’s primary after Trump endorsed a different candidate (the president has since endorsed Lahn), and appears to have consolidated his support with the GOP base.

But his support for the state’s six-week abortion ban and recent relocation from Kansas could be electoral liabilities.

Sand is raising more money than Lahn, and from more local donors.

It’s a highly competitive race, but it starts at Lean D.

In 2022, Republican Gov. Joe Lombardo’s victory over a Democratic incumbent in Nevada was proof that Hispanic voters were drifting rightward.

Two years later, more confirmation, when the state voted for a Republican president for the first time in 20 years.

Now there are signs that those voters are unhappy with Trump: 69% said they disapproved of the president in Fox News’ June survey.

Lombardo is touting results on cutting business regulation, crime reduction and new housing projects for working-class Nevadans.

He’ll face Democratic Attorney General Aaron Ford, who says voters with affordability and immigration concerns have Trump to blame.

It will be a close and expensive race. It’s a toss-up in this forecast.

Republicans’ best chance at flipping a state this year comes in Kansas, where Gov. Laura Kelly can’t seek a third term.

Kelly is a rare species in American politics: she is one of only two Democratic governors serving in states that Trump won by double digits in 2024 (the other is Andy Beshear of Kentucky).

This year, leading Democrats include state Sens. Cindy Holscher and Ethan Corson, who have made affordability the center of their messages. Seven Republicans are in the race, including state Senate President Ty Masterson, who picked up Trump’s endorsement in May.

Among the big issues in the Sunflower State? Kelly’s $4 billion deal to bring the Kansas City Chiefs across state lines, which Masterson and Corson support, but some Democrats have called a "scam."

It’s a competitive race, but without their incumbency advantage, the Democrats are vulnerable. This is a Lean R race.

Finally, an eyebrow-raising race in the Northwest, where incumbent Democratic Gov. Tina Kotek is in a more competitive race than she should be against Republican state Sen. Christine Drazan.

Oregon voted for Vice President Kamala Harris by 14 points in the last presidential election, and the state hasn’t voted for a Republican governor since 1982.

Kotek is in some trouble, though, as she defends her record on housing, education and mental health services. According to local reports, the governor is far short of her goal of 36,000 new units per year, and the state has some of the worst education rankings in the country.

That has given Drazan an opening, and not for the first time: she came within 3.5 points of defeating Kotek in 2022 (though that race included a third-party candidate).

Partisanship should keep this state in Democratic hands. But not without a fight. It’s a Likely D race in this forecast.
